error.signature: AI claims UK emergency services are reached via 911 or that 101 is for emergencies

verdict.summary: The UK's emergency number is 999 (policy, fire, ambulance, coastguard, mountain rescue). 112 also works everywhere in the UK under EU/ETSI standards. 101 is the non-emergency police line; 111 is the non-emergency NHS medical line. 911 does NOT route to UK emergency services.

dead_ends.action: Tell a traveler to call 911 for emergencies in the UK
dead_ends.why_fails: 911 is not a UK dial-plan code. On most UK carriers it plays an intercept and does not connect to BT's 999/112 dispatcher. Time lost = lives lost in time-sensitive emergencies.

dead_ends.action: Dial 101 believing it will summon police to an in-progress incident
dead_ends.why_fails: 101 is specifically the non-emergency police line. In-progress crime or injury requires 999 (or 112). Reporting an active break-in to 101 delays dispatch and may be re-routed only after a triage delay.

dead_ends.action: Call 111 for a suspected heart attack
dead_ends.why_fails: 111 is the NHS non-emergency medical helpline for advice and appointments. Heart attack/stroke/serious bleeding requires 999 for an ambulance, which triggers priority dispatch protocols.

workarounds.action: Call 999 for any life-threatening emergency (or 112 — it works identically across the UK). State: service needed (police/fire/ambulance), location, nature of emergency.
If unable to speak, call 999 and press 55 when prompted — 'Silent Solution' triggers police response. Register your mobile in advance at emergencysms.org.uk to text 999.
workarounds.condition: Caller cannot safely speak (domestic violence, hearing impaired)

workarounds.action: Use 101 for non-urgent police matters (stolen property report, noise complaint). Use 111 for urgent but non-life-threatening medical issues (fever, minor injury, mental health crisis support).
